subject: Chemistry
topic: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
text: RATE OF REACTION:- For a reaction R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} P Rate of reaction = change of conc. of R or {tex}\frac {P}{Time \ interval}{/tex} Rate = {tex}\frac {-\Delta[{R}]}{\Delta t} = \frac {\Delta[{P}]}{ \Delta {t}}{/tex} , This is average rate of reaction. For expressing the rate of such a reaction where stoichiometric coefficients of reactants or products are not equal to one, rate of disappearance of any of the reactants or the rate of appearance of products is divided by their respective stoichiometiric coefficients. For e.g. for a chemical reaction: N 2 (g) + 3H 2 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NH 3 (g) r avg = {tex}-\Delta{/tex} [N 2 ] {tex}\Delta {t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-1}{3}{/tex} {tex}(\frac {\Delta [H_2]}{\Delta t}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {1}{ 2} (\frac {\Delta [NH_3]} {\Delta t}){/tex}
text_html:
RATE OF REACTION:-
For a reaction R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} P
Rate of reaction = change of conc. of R or {tex}\frac {P}{Time \ interval}{/tex}
Rate = {tex}\frac {-\Delta[{R}]}{\Delta t} = \frac {\Delta[{P}]}{ \Delta {t}}{/tex}, This is average rate of reaction.
For expressing the rate of such a reaction where stoichiometric coefficients of reactants or products are not equal to one, rate of disappearance of any of the reactants or the rate of appearance of products is divided by their respective stoichiometiric coefficients.
For e.g. for a chemical reaction: N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NH3(g)
ravg = {tex}-\Delta{/tex}[N2]{tex}\Delta {t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-1}{3}{/tex}{tex}(\frac {\Delta [H_2]}{\Delta t}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {1}{ 2} (\frac {\Delta [NH_3]} {\Delta t}){/tex}

subject: Chemistry
topic: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
text: Units of rate of a reaction:- Concentration time -1 if concentration is in mol L -1 and time is in seconds then the units will be mol L -1 s -1 . In gaseous reaction, when the concentration of the gases is expressed in terms of their partial pressure, then the units will be atm s -1 .
text_html:
Units of rate of a reaction:-
Concentration time-1

  • if concentration is in mol L-1 and time is in seconds then the units will be mol L-1 s-1.
  • In gaseous reaction, when the concentration of the gases is expressed in terms of their partial pressure, then the units will be atm s-1.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
text: Types of Reaction Speeds Some reactions such as ionic reactions occur very fast, like the precipitation of silver chloride. Conversely, reactions such as rusting of iron are very slow, while others like the inversion of cane sugar proceed at a moderate speed.
text_html: Types of Reaction Speeds Some reactions such as ionic reactions occur very fast, like the precipitation of silver chloride. Conversely, reactions such as rusting of iron are very slow, while others like the inversion of cane sugar proceed at a moderate speed.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
text: Definition of Reaction Rate The rate of a reaction is defined as the change in concentration of a reactant or product in unit time. This can be expressed by: The rate of decrease in concentration of reactants The rate of increase in concentration of products
text_html:
Definition of Reaction Rate The rate of a reaction is defined as the change in concentration of a reactant or product in unit time. This can be expressed by:
  • The rate of decrease in concentration of reactants
  • The rate of increase in concentration of products

subject: Chemistry
topic: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
text: Reaction Rate Calculation For a hypothetical reaction {tex} {R} \rightarrow {P} {/tex} : Rate of disappearance of R = - {tex}\frac{\Delta[{R}]}{\Delta t}{/tex} Rate of appearance of P = {tex}\frac{\Delta[{P}]}{\Delta t}{/tex}
text_html:
Reaction Rate Calculation For a hypothetical reaction {tex} {R} \rightarrow {P} {/tex}:

  • Rate of disappearance of R = -{tex}\frac{\Delta[{R}]}{\Delta t}{/tex}
  • Rate of appearance of P = {tex}\frac{\Delta[{P}]}{\Delta t}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
topic: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
text: Significance of Negative Rate Sign The negative sign in the rate of disappearance of reactants is used to make the rate of reaction a positive value, indicating the rate of change.
text_html: Significance of Negative Rate Sign The negative sign in the rate of disappearance of reactants is used to make the rate of reaction a positive value, indicating the rate of change.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
text: Average Reaction Rate The average rate is based on the change in concentration of reactants or products over time. It is calculated over an interval and will not predict instantaneous rates.
text_html: Average Reaction Rate The average rate is based on the change in concentration of reactants or products over time. It is calculated over an interval and will not predict instantaneous rates.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
text: Instantaneous Reaction Rate Instantaneous rate is determined by taking the average rate over an infinitesimally small time interval using: Graphical tangent method on the concentration vs. time curve
text_html:
Instantaneous Reaction Rate Instantaneous rate is determined by taking the average rate over an infinitesimally small time interval using:
  • Graphical tangent method on the concentration vs. time curve

subject: Chemistry
topic: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
text: Units of Reaction Rate The units of reaction rate depend on the units of concentration and time. For concentration in mol L -1 and time in s, the unit is mol L -1 s -1 .
text_html: Units of Reaction Rate The units of reaction rate depend on the units of concentration and time. For concentration in mol L-1 and time in s, the unit is mol L-1 s-1.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
text: Stoichiometric Coefficients in Reaction Rates For reactions with differing stoichiometry, the rate of the reaction is adjusted by dividing the rate of change by their respective coefficients, ensuring consistent comparison.
text_html: Stoichiometric Coefficients in Reaction Rates For reactions with differing stoichiometry, the rate of the reaction is adjusted by dividing the rate of change by their respective coefficients, ensuring consistent comparison.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
text: Effect of Stoichiometry on Rate In reactions like 2 HI (g) \rightarrow H 2 (g) + I 2 (g), the rate of consumption of HI is twice the rate of product formation.
text_html: Effect of Stoichiometry on Rate In reactions like 2 HI (g) \rightarrow H2 (g) + I2 (g), the rate of consumption of HI is twice the rate of product formation.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
text: Gaseous Reaction Rates In gaseous reactions at constant temperature, reaction rates can be expressed in terms of changes in partial pressures due to the direct proportionality of concentration and pressure.
text_html: Gaseous Reaction Rates In gaseous reactions at constant temperature, reaction rates can be expressed in terms of changes in partial pressures due to the direct proportionality of concentration and pressure.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: Factors affecting rate of a reaction: Nature of the reacting species: Chemical reaction is a process in which new. Concentration of reactants: The rate of reaction increases with increase in concentration of reactants. Effect of temperature: The rate of reaction is nearly double for every 10 o C rise in temperature. Catalyst: generally catalyst increases the rate of reaction. Effect of radiations: The rates of some reactions are enhanced due to absorption of radiation. These reactions are called photochemical reactions.
text_html:
Factors affecting rate of a reaction:

  1. Nature of the reacting species: Chemical reaction is a process in which new.
  2. Concentration of reactants: The rate of reaction increases with increase in concentration of reactants.
  3. Effect of temperature: The rate of reaction is nearly double for every 10oC rise in temperature.
  4. Catalyst: generally catalyst increases the rate of reaction.
  5. Effect of radiations: The rates of some reactions are enhanced due to absorption of radiation. These reactions are called photochemical reactions.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: RATE CONSTANT OF A REACTION: at a given temperature may be defined as rate of the reaction when the molar concentration of each of the reactants is unity.
text_html: RATE CONSTANT OF A REACTION: at a given temperature may be defined as rate of the reaction when the molar concentration of each of the reactants is unity.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: CHARACTERISTICS OF RATE CONSTANT Rate constant is a measure of the rate of the reaction. Larger the value of k, faster is the reaction. Different reactions have different values of k. For a particular reaction, the rate constant is independent of concentration. At a particular temperature, the value of k is constant. However, it changes with temperature.
text_html:
CHARACTERISTICS OF RATE CONSTANT

  1. Rate constant is a measure of the rate of the reaction.
  2. Larger the value of k, faster is the reaction.
  3. Different reactions have different values of k.
  4. For a particular reaction, the rate constant is independent of concentration.
  5. At a particular temperature, the value of k is constant. However, it changes with temperature.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: Rate of Reaction Dependence The rate of a chemical reaction is influenced by several experimental conditions, including the concentration of reactants, temperature, and the presence of a catalyst.
text_html:
Rate of Reaction Dependence
The rate of a chemical reaction is influenced by several experimental conditions, including the concentration of reactants, temperature, and the presence of a catalyst.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: Rate Law and Reaction Concentration The rate law or rate expression relates the rate of reaction to the concentration of reactants, often in the form Rate = k[A]^x[B]^y. Here, k is the rate constant, and x and y are the exponents specific to each reactant.
text_html:
Rate Law and Reaction Concentration
The rate law or rate expression relates the rate of reaction to the concentration of reactants, often in the form Rate = k[A]^x[B]^y. Here, k is the rate constant, and x and y are the exponents specific to each reactant.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: Differential Rate Equation The rate of a reaction is adjusted for the concentration decrease of reactants over time as per the differential rate equation -d[R]/dt = k[A]^x[B]^y. The rate decreases as the concentration of reactants diminishes.
text_html:
Differential Rate Equation
The rate of a reaction is adjusted for the concentration decrease of reactants over time as per the differential rate equation -d[R]/dt = k[A]^x[B]^y. The rate decreases as the concentration of reactants diminishes.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: Reaction Rate Experiment Experiments show that changes in reactant concentrations affect the rate. For example, doubling the concentration of NO in a reaction increases the rate by a factor of four.
text_html:
Reaction Rate Experiment
Experiments show that changes in reactant concentrations affect the rate. For example, doubling the concentration of NO in a reaction increases the rate by a factor of four.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: Order of Reaction The order of a reaction is determined by the sum of the exponents in the rate law. It signifies how changes in reactant concentrations impact the reaction rate. Possible orders include 0, 1, 2, 3, or fractions.
text_html:
Order of Reaction
The order of a reaction is determined by the sum of the exponents in the rate law. It signifies how changes in reactant concentrations impact the reaction rate. Possible orders include 0, 1, 2, 3, or fractions.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: Elementary vs. Complex Reactions Elementary reactions occur in one step, while complex reactions require multiple steps and are influenced by intermediate species. The stoichiometric equation doesn't always reflect the reaction order.
text_html:
Elementary vs. Complex Reactions
Elementary reactions occur in one step, while complex reactions require multiple steps and are influenced by intermediate species. The stoichiometric equation doesn't always reflect the reaction order.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: Units of Rate Constant Zero order: mol L^-1 s^-1 First order: s^-1 Second order: mol^-1 L s^-1 The units of the rate constant depend on the overall order of the reaction.
text_html:
Units of Rate Constant
  • Zero order: mol L^-1 s^-1
  • First order: s^-1
  • Second order: mol^-1 L s^-1
The units of the rate constant depend on the overall order of the reaction.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: Molecularity Molecularity refers to the number of reacting species in an elementary reaction step. It helps in understanding reaction mechanisms and can be unimolecular, bimolecular, or trimolecular.
text_html:
Molecularity
Molecularity refers to the number of reacting species in an elementary reaction step. It helps in understanding reaction mechanisms and can be unimolecular, bimolecular, or trimolecular.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: Rate Determining Step In a complex reaction, the slowest step is the rate determining step and dictates the overall reaction rate. This concept helps to understand reaction mechanisms and overall order.
text_html:
Rate Determining Step
In a complex reaction, the slowest step is the rate determining step and dictates the overall reaction rate. This concept helps to understand reaction mechanisms and overall order.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Factors Influencing Rate of a Reaction
text: Key Differences: Order vs. Molecularity Order: determined experimentally, can be non-integers. Molecularity: must be an integer, applicable only to elementary reactions.
text_html:
Key Differences: Order vs. Molecularity
  • Order: determined experimentally, can be non-integers.
  • Molecularity: must be an integer, applicable only to elementary reactions.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: LAW OF MASS ACTION: The rate of a chemical reaction is directly proportional to the product of the molar concentrations of the reactants. According to law of mass action aA + bB {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Products Rate {tex}\alpha{/tex} [A]a[B] b , Rate = k[A] a [B] b
text_html:
LAW OF MASS ACTION:
The rate of a chemical reaction is directly proportional to the product of the molar concentrations of the reactants.
According to law of mass action
aA + bB {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Products
Rate {tex}\alpha{/tex}[A]a[B]b,
Rate = k[A]a[B]b

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: RATE LAW is the expression in which reaction rate is given in terms of molar concentration of reactants with each term raised to some power, which may or may not be same as the stoichiometric coefficient of the reacting species in a balanced chemical equation. It is determined experimentally.
text_html: RATE LAW is the expression in which reaction rate is given in terms of molar concentration of reactants with each term raised to some power, which may or may not be same as the stoichiometric coefficient of the reacting species in a balanced chemical equation. It is determined experimentally.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: ORDER OF A REACTION: The sum of powers of the concentration of the reactants in the rate law expression is called the order of the chemical reaction. For the rate law expression Rate = k[A] x [b] y Order = x + y
text_html:
ORDER OF A REACTION: The sum of powers of the concentration of the reactants in the rate law expression is called the order of the chemical reaction.
For the rate law expression
Rate = k[A]x[b]y
Order = x + y

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: UNITS OF RATE CONSTANTS: Units of rate constant are different from reaction of different order: (moIL -1 ) 1-n time -1 where 'n' is order of reaction. For gas phase reaction unit of rate constant is (atm of bar) 1-n s -1
text_html: UNITS OF RATE CONSTANTS: Units of rate constant are different from reaction of different order: (moIL-1)1-n time-1 where 'n' is order of reaction. For gas phase reaction unit of rate constant is (atm of bar)1-ns-1

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: Reaction Order ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex} ) Units of rate constant Zero order reaction 0 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{molL}^{-1}}{\mathrm{~s}} \times \frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{molL}^{-1}\right)^0}{/tex} = molL -1 s -1 First order reaction 1 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{molL}^{-1}}{\mathrm{~s}} \times \frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{molL}^{-1}\right)^1}=\mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} Second order reaction 2 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{molL}^{-1}}{\mathrm{~s}} \times \frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{molL}^{-1}\right)^2}{/tex} = Lmol -1 s -1
text_html:
Reaction Order ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex}) Units of rate constant
Zero order reaction 0 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{molL}^{-1}}{\mathrm{~s}} \times \frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{molL}^{-1}\right)^0}{/tex} = molL-1s-1
First order reaction 1 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{molL}^{-1}}{\mathrm{~s}} \times \frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{molL}^{-1}\right)^1}=\mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
Second order reaction 2 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{molL}^{-1}}{\mathrm{~s}} \times \frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{molL}^{-1}\right)^2}{/tex} = Lmol-1s-1

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: MECHANISMAND RATE LAW: The reactions taking place in one step are called elementary reactions. When a sequence of elementary reactions, (called mechanism) gives us the products, the reactions are called Complex reactions. In complex reactions, the rate of the reaction is determined by the slowest step in the sequence. The slowest step is called rate determining step in the proposed mechanism.
text_html:
MECHANISMAND RATE LAW:
The reactions taking place in one step are called elementary reactions. When a sequence of elementary reactions, (called mechanism) gives us the products, the reactions are called Complex reactions. In complex reactions, the rate of the reaction is determined by the slowest step in the sequence. The slowest step is called rate determining step in the proposed mechanism.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: ZERO ORDER REACTION Integrated rate equation k = {tex}\frac {[R]0 - [R]}{t}{/tex}
text_html:
ZERO ORDER REACTION
Integrated rate equation k = {tex}\frac {[R]0 - [R]}{t}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: INTEGRATED RATE EQUATION FOR FIRST ORDER REACTION: {tex}\mathrm{k}=\frac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \frac{[\mathrm{R}]_0}{[\mathrm{R}]}{/tex} where [R] 0 is initial concentration of reactants and [R] is concentration at time t. For a typical first order gas phase reaction: A(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B(g) + C(g) {tex}k=\frac{2.303 \log }{t} \frac{P_i}{\left(2 P_1-P_t\right)}{/tex} Where p i is the initial pressure of A and P t the total pressure at time 't'.
text_html:
INTEGRATED RATE EQUATION FOR FIRST ORDER REACTION:
{tex}\mathrm{k}=\frac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \frac{[\mathrm{R}]_0}{[\mathrm{R}]}{/tex}
where [R]0 is initial concentration of reactants and [R] is concentration at time t.

For a typical first order gas phase reaction: A(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B(g) + C(g)
{tex}k=\frac{2.303 \log }{t} \frac{P_i}{\left(2 P_1-P_t\right)}{/tex}
Where pi is the initial pressure of A and Pt the total pressure at time 't'.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: HALF-LIFE PERIOD (t 1/2 ): The half-life of a reaction is the time in which the concentration of a reactant is reduced to one half of its initial concentration. For zero order reaction: t 1/2 = {tex}\frac {[R]_0}{2K}{/tex} For first order reaction: t 1/2 = {tex}\frac {0.693}{K}{/tex} For zero order reaction t 1/2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} [R] 0 . For first order reaction t 1/2 is independent of [R] 0 .
text_html:
HALF-LIFE PERIOD (t1/2): The half-life of a reaction is the time in which the concentration of a reactant is reduced to one half of its initial concentration.
For zero order reaction: t1/2 = {tex}\frac {[R]_0}{2K}{/tex}
For first order reaction: t1/2 = {tex}\frac {0.693}{K}{/tex}
For zero order reaction t1/2 {tex}\alpha{/tex}[R]0. For first order reaction t1/2 is independent of [R]0.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: Rate Equations and Reaction Order The concentration dependence of reaction rate is termed the differential rate equation. Instantaneous rates are measured by the slope of the tangent on a concentration vs time plot.
text_html:
Rate Equations and Reaction Order
  • The concentration dependence of reaction rate is termed the differential rate equation.
  • Instantaneous rates are measured by the slope of the tangent on a concentration vs time plot.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: Integrated Rate Equation This is derived from the differential rate equation. It relates experimental data like concentrations at different times and the rate constant.
text_html:
Integrated Rate Equation
  • This is derived from the differential rate equation.
  • It relates experimental data like concentrations at different times and the rate constant.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: Zero Order Reactions Rate of reaction is independent of reactant concentration. Formula: Rate = -d[R]/dt = k. Occurs under special conditions, e.g., some enzyme-catalyzed reactions. Includes the decomposition of gaseous ammonia on a platinum surface.
text_html:
Zero Order Reactions
  • Rate of reaction is independent of reactant concentration.
  • Formula: Rate = -d[R]/dt = k.
  • Occurs under special conditions, e.g., some enzyme-catalyzed reactions.
  • Includes the decomposition of gaseous ammonia on a platinum surface.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: Zero Order Reaction Graph [R] plotted against t yields a straight line. Slope = -k and intercept = [R] 0 .
text_html:
Zero Order Reaction Graph
  • [R] plotted against t yields a straight line.
  • Slope = -k and intercept = [R]0.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: First Order Reactions Reaction rate is proportional to the concentration of one reactant. Examples: Radioactive decay, hydrogenation of ethene. Formula: Rate = -d[R]/dt = k[R].
text_html:
First Order Reactions
  • Reaction rate is proportional to the concentration of one reactant.
  • Examples: Radioactive decay, hydrogenation of ethene.
  • Formula: Rate = -d[R]/dt = k[R].

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: First Order Reaction Graph ln[R] plotted against t also yields a straight line. Slope = -k and intercept = ln[R] 0 .
text_html:
First Order Reaction Graph
  • ln[R] plotted against t also yields a straight line.
  • Slope = -k and intercept = ln[R]0.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: First Order Kinetics - Calculations Formula: k = (1/t) ln([R] 0 /[R]). Integrated equation: [R] = [R] 0 e -kt .
text_html:
First Order Kinetics - Calculations
  • Formula: k = (1/t) ln([R]0/[R]).
  • Integrated equation: [R] = [R]0 e-kt.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: Half-Life for Reactions Zero Order: t 1/2 = [R] 0 /(2k), dependent on initial concentration. First Order: t 1/2 = 0.693/k, independent of initial concentration.
text_html:
Half-Life for Reactions
  • Zero Order: t1/2 = [R]0/(2k), dependent on initial concentration.
  • First Order: t1/2 = 0.693/k, independent of initial concentration.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: Analyzing Reaction Data Zero Order: [R] vs t. First Order: ln [R] vs t, or log([R] 0 /[R]) vs t (slope = k/2.303).
text_html:
Analyzing Reaction Data
  • Zero Order: [R] vs t.
  • First Order: ln [R] vs t, or log([R]0/[R]) vs t (slope = k/2.303).

subject: Chemistry
topic: Integrated Rate Equations
text: Pseudo First Order Reactions Hydrolysis of ethyl acetate and inversion of cane sugar behave as first-order reactions under certain conditions. Reagents in large excess cause one reactant's concentration to dominate the rate-law expression.
text_html:
Pseudo First Order Reactions
  • Hydrolysis of ethyl acetate and inversion of cane sugar behave as first-order reactions under certain conditions.
  • Reagents in large excess cause one reactant's concentration to dominate the rate-law expression.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Temperature Dependence of The Rate of A Reaction
text: Temperature coefficient: the ration of rate constant at two temperatures having difference of 100 C is called temperature coefficient. Temperature coefficient = Rate constant at T + {tex}\frac {10^oC} {Rate \ constant \ at \ T^oC}{/tex}
text_html:
Temperature coefficient: the ration of rate constant at two temperatures having difference of 100 C is called temperature coefficient.
Temperature coefficient = Rate constant at T + {tex}\frac {10^oC} {Rate \ constant \ at \ T^oC}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
topic: Temperature Dependence of The Rate of A Reaction
text: Effect of Temperature on Chemical Reactions Most chemical reactions speed up with an increase in temperature. For example: Decomposition of {tex} {N}_2 {O}_5 {/tex} : 12 min at {tex} 50^{\circ} {C} {/tex} , 5 h at {tex} 25^{\circ} {C} {/tex} , and 10 days at {tex} 0^{\circ} {C} {/tex} . Potassium permanganate and oxalic acid reaction happens faster at a higher temperature.
text_html:
Effect of Temperature on Chemical Reactions
Most chemical reactions speed up with an increase in temperature. For example:

  • Decomposition of {tex} {N}_2 {O}_5 {/tex}: 12 min at {tex} 50^{\circ} {C} {/tex}, 5 h at {tex} 25^{\circ} {C} {/tex}, and 10 days at {tex} 0^{\circ} {C} {/tex}.
  • Potassium permanganate and oxalic acid reaction happens faster at a higher temperature.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Temperature Dependence of The Rate of A Reaction
text: Arrhenius Equation The temperature dependence of reaction rates can be explained by the Arrhenius equation: Equation: {tex} k={A} {e}^{-{Ea} / RT} {/tex} {tex} A {/tex} is the frequency factor, a constant specific to a certain reaction. {tex} R {/tex} is the gas constant, and {tex} E_{{a}} {/tex} is the activation energy.
text_html:
Arrhenius Equation
The temperature dependence of reaction rates can be explained by the Arrhenius equation:

  • Equation: {tex} k={A} {e}^{-{Ea} / RT} {/tex}
  • {tex} A {/tex} is the frequency factor, a constant specific to a certain reaction.
  • {tex} R {/tex} is the gas constant, and {tex} E_{{a}} {/tex} is the activation energy.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Temperature Dependence of The Rate of A Reaction
text: Activation Energy and Activated Complex High activation energy signifies the energy required to form an unstable intermediate, called the activated complex. This complex exists briefly before turning into products.
text_html:
Activation Energy and Activated Complex
High activation energy signifies the energy required to form an unstable intermediate, called the activated complex. This complex exists briefly before turning into products.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Temperature Dependence of The Rate of A Reaction
text: Arrhenius Plot and Rate Constant Taking the natural log of the Arrhenius equation: Plotting {tex} \ln k {/tex} vs {tex} 1/T {/tex} yields a straight line. Increased temperature or decreased activation energy results in a higher rate of reaction.
text_html:
Arrhenius Plot and Rate Constant
Taking the natural log of the Arrhenius equation:

  • Plotting {tex} \ln k {/tex} vs {tex} 1/T {/tex} yields a straight line.
  • Increased temperature or decreased activation energy results in a higher rate of reaction.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Temperature Dependence of The Rate of A Reaction
text: Temperature Impact on Arrhenius Equation For a 10°C increase: The rate constant approximately doubles. The factor {tex} {e}^{-E_a/RT} {/tex} correlates to the fraction of molecules with kinetic energy above {tex} E_a {/tex} .
text_html:
Temperature Impact on Arrhenius Equation
For a 10°C increase:

  • The rate constant approximately doubles.
  • The factor {tex} {e}^{-E_a/RT} {/tex} correlates to the fraction of molecules with kinetic energy above {tex} E_a {/tex}.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Temperature Dependence of The Rate of A Reaction
text: Kinetic Energy Distribution Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution describes kinetic energy profiles. Raising temperature broadens the distribution curve and increases the fraction of molecules with higher energies.
text_html:
Kinetic Energy Distribution
  • Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution describes kinetic energy profiles.
  • Raising temperature broadens the distribution curve and increases the fraction of molecules with higher energies.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Temperature Dependence of The Rate of A Reaction
text: Maxwell-Boltzmann Distribution The peak of the curve signifies the most probable kinetic energy, decreasing beyond this point. Increased temperature shifts this peak to higher energies.
text_html:
Maxwell-Boltzmann Distribution
The peak of the curve signifies the most probable kinetic energy, decreasing beyond this point. Increased temperature shifts this peak to higher energies.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Temperature Dependence of The Rate of A Reaction
text: Effect of Catalyst A catalyst accelerates the reaction rate by providing an alternative route with reduced activation energy: Does not alter Gibbs energy or equilibrium constant. Works by forming an intermediate complex with reactants.
text_html:
Effect of Catalyst
A catalyst accelerates the reaction rate by providing an alternative route with reduced activation energy:
  • Does not alter Gibbs energy or equilibrium constant.
  • Works by forming an intermediate complex with reactants.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Temperature Dependence of The Rate of A Reaction
text: Using Arrhenius Equation to Identify Constants From the slope ( {tex} -\frac{E_{a}}{R} {/tex} ) and intercept ( {tex} \ln A {/tex} ) of an Arrhenius plot: Calculate activation energy and pre-exponential factor.
text_html:
Using Arrhenius Equation to Identify Constants
From the slope ({tex} -\frac{E_{a}}{R} {/tex}) and intercept ({tex} \ln A {/tex}) of an Arrhenius plot:

  • Calculate activation energy and pre-exponential factor.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Temperature Dependence of The Rate of A Reaction
text: Catalyst Characteristics A catalyst doesn't participate permanently in the reaction. A small quantity can affect large amounts of reactants. Catalysts do not influence reaction spontaneity.
text_html:
Catalyst Characteristics
  • A catalyst doesn't participate permanently in the reaction.
  • A small quantity can affect large amounts of reactants.
  • Catalysts do not influence reaction spontaneity.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Collision theory of Bimolecular Gaseous Reactions
text: Activation energy: The minimum extra amount of energy absorbed by reactant molecules so that their energy becomes equal to the threshold energy is called activation energy. Activation energy = Threshold energy - Kinetic energy
text_html:
Activation energy: The minimum extra amount of energy absorbed by reactant molecules so that their energy becomes equal to the threshold energy is called activation energy.
Activation energy = Threshold energy - Kinetic energy

subject: Chemistry
topic: Collision theory of Bimolecular Gaseous Reactions
text: Arhenius Equation: K = Ae -Ea/RT where, K = Rate constant A = Arrhenius energy (Frequency factor or pre-exponential factor) Ea = Activation energy R = Rate constant T = Temperature = Fraction of molecules having energy equal to or more than activation energy log K = log A - {tex}\frac {Ea}{RT}{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{\mathrm{K}_2}{\mathrm{~K}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{Ea}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{\mathrm{K}_2}{\mathrm{~K}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{\mathrm{T}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_2}-\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right]{/tex} Where R = 8.314 JK -1 mol -1
text_html:
Arhenius Equation:
K = Ae-Ea/RT
where, K = Rate constant
A = Arrhenius energy (Frequency factor or pre-exponential factor)
Ea = Activation energy
R = Rate constant
T = Temperature
= Fraction of molecules having energy equal to or more than activation energy
log K = log A - {tex}\frac {Ea}{RT}{/tex}
{tex}\log \frac{\mathrm{K}_2}{\mathrm{~K}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{Ea}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\log \frac{\mathrm{K}_2}{\mathrm{~K}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{\mathrm{T}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_2}-\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right]{/tex} Where R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1

subject: Chemistry
topic: Collision theory of Bimolecular Gaseous Reactions
text: Collision Theory Fundamentals Collision theory provides insight into reactions, established by Max Trautz and William Lewis around 1916-18, enhancing the understanding of reaction energies and mechanisms using the kinetic theory of gases.
text_html:
Collision Theory Fundamentals
Collision theory provides insight into reactions, established by Max Trautz and William Lewis around 1916-18, enhancing the understanding of reaction energies and mechanisms using the kinetic theory of gases.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Collision theory of Bimolecular Gaseous Reactions
text: Concept of Collision Frequency Reactant molecules are considered hard spheres, and reactions occur upon collisions. Collision frequency \(Z\) is defined as the number of collisions per second per unit volume.
text_html:
Concept of Collision Frequency
Reactant molecules are considered hard spheres, and reactions occur upon collisions. Collision frequency \(Z\) is defined as the number of collisions per second per unit volume.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Collision theory of Bimolecular Gaseous Reactions
text: Role of Activation Energy Activation energy {tex}E_a{/tex} impacts reaction rate. The formula for bimolecular elementary reactions: {tex}\text{Rate}=Z_{AB} e^{-E_{a}/RT}{/tex}
text_html:
Role of Activation Energy

  • Activation energy {tex}E_a{/tex} impacts reaction rate.
  • The formula for bimolecular elementary reactions: {tex}\text{Rate}=Z_{AB} e^{-E_{a}/RT}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
topic: Collision theory of Bimolecular Gaseous Reactions
text: Effective Collisions Not all collisions result in product formation. Effective collisions require sufficient kinetic energy (threshold energy) and proper orientation.
text_html:
Effective Collisions
  • Not all collisions result in product formation.
  • Effective collisions require sufficient kinetic energy (threshold energy) and proper orientation.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Collision theory of Bimolecular Gaseous Reactions
text: Probability or Steric Factor (P) Introduced to account for the correct orientation of molecules during collisions, modifying the collision theory rate equation to: {tex}\text{Rate}=P Z_{AB} e^{-E_{a}/RT}{/tex}
text_html:
Probability or Steric Factor (P)
Introduced to account for the correct orientation of molecules during collisions, modifying the collision theory rate equation to: {tex}\text{Rate}=P Z_{AB} e^{-E_{a}/RT}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
topic: Collision theory of Bimolecular Gaseous Reactions
text: Limitations of Collision Theory Consideration of molecules as hard spheres leads to misconceptions, overlooking structural aspects, with further details in advanced studies.
text_html:
Limitations of Collision Theory
Consideration of molecules as hard spheres leads to misconceptions, overlooking structural aspects, with further details in advanced studies.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Collision theory of Bimolecular Gaseous Reactions
text: Chemical Kinetics Overview Concerned with reaction rates, influencing factors, atom rearrangement, and intermediate formation, determined by both instantaneous and average rates.
text_html:
Chemical Kinetics Overview
Concerned with reaction rates, influencing factors, atom rearrangement, and intermediate formation, determined by both instantaneous and average rates.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Collision theory of Bimolecular Gaseous Reactions
text: Reaction Rate and Rate Law Rate law represents reaction rate, experimentally determined. Order of reaction equals concentration power in the rate law equation.
text_html:
Reaction Rate and Rate Law
  • Rate law represents reaction rate, experimentally determined.
  • Order of reaction equals concentration power in the rate law equation.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Collision theory of Bimolecular Gaseous Reactions
text: Molecularity and Order In elementary reactions, molecularity is limited to 1-3, while order can extend from 0 to fractions. Molecularity equals order for these reactions.
text_html:
Molecularity and Order
In elementary reactions, molecularity is limited to 1-3, while order can extend from 0 to fractions. Molecularity equals order for these reactions.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Collision theory of Bimolecular Gaseous Reactions
text: Arrhenius Equation and Reaction Rates {tex}k=A e^{-E_a/RT}{/tex} Increasing temperature or lowering {tex}E_a{/tex} boosts reaction rates. Presence of catalysts lowers activation energy, providing alternative reaction pathways.
text_html:
Arrhenius Equation and Reaction Rates

  • {tex}k=A e^{-E_a/RT}{/tex}
  • Increasing temperature or lowering {tex}E_a{/tex} boosts reaction rates.
  • Presence of catalysts lowers activation energy, providing alternative reaction pathways.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Pseudo First Order Reaction
text: PSEUDO FIRST ORDER REACTIONS: Chemical reactions which are not truly of the first order but under certain conditions become first order reactions are called e.g. A bimolecular reaction, in which one reactant is present in large excess and rate of reaction is independent of its concentration, the reaction follows first order kinetics. For example, ester hydrolysis, where water is taken in excess. CH 3 COOCH 2 CH 3 + H 2 O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CH 3 COOH + CH 3 CH 2 OH is a bimolecular but first order reaction.
text_html:
PSEUDO FIRST ORDER REACTIONS: Chemical reactions which are not truly of the first order but under certain conditions become first order reactions are called e.g. A bimolecular reaction, in which one reactant is present in large excess and rate of reaction is independent of its concentration, the reaction follows first order kinetics.
For example, ester hydrolysis, where water is taken in excess.
CH3COOCH2CH3 + H2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CH3COOH + CH3CH2OH is a bimolecular but first order reaction.
